channel.title: rylanpeng.dev
channel.description: A technical blog about Robotics, Embedded Systems, and AI.

item.title: Yocto Basics Part 3: Kernel, SystemD & Tooling (Notes from Tech-A-Byte)
item.description:
Yocto Basics Part 3: Kernel, SystemD & Tooling (Notes from Tech-A-Byte)

Part 3 covers the more advanced territory: writing SystemD service recipes, configuring the kernel via menuconfig, adding kernel modules (both out-of-tree and in-tree), reusing logic with .bbclass, iterating with devtool, and customizing your distribution.

item.title: Yocto Basics Part 2: Writing Recipes (Notes from Tech-A-Byte)
item.description:
Yocto Basics Part 2: Writing Recipes (Notes from Tech-A-Byte)

Part 2 covers writing .bb recipes from scratch: the six standard tasks that every recipe goes through, how to declare build and runtime dependencies, how to use Makefiles via oe_runmake, and how to extend or mask recipes without touching the originals.

item.title: Yocto Basics Part 1: Fundamentals (Notes from Tech-A-Byte)
item.description:
Yocto Basics Part 1: Fundamentals (Notes from Tech-A-Byte)

A running set of notes from working through the Tech-A-Byte Yocto lecture series. Part 1 covers the core configuration files, the layer/recipe hierarchy, key directory variables, and BitBake assignment syntax, which is the foundation everything else builds on.

item.title: Build demo-image-weston for Jetson Orin Nano
item.description:
Build demo-image-weston for Jetson Orin Nano

demo-image-weston is the first image in the tegra-demo-distro ladder where DisplayPort works out of the box.

This post walks through the host setup, Yocto build, flash flow, and first boot checks for a Jetson Orin Nano booting from NVMe.

item.title: Talk to SSD1306 using SPI on Jetson Orin Nano
item.description:
Talk to SSD1306 using SPI on Jetson Orin Nano

Driving a small OLED display over SPI from the Jetson Orin Nano's 40-pin header. This covers hardware wiring, device-tree overlay configuration, and a Python test script.

item.title: Biped Robot RL Simulation on Jetson Orin Nano
item.description:
Biped Robot RL Simulation on Jetson Orin Nano

Training a biped robot to walk using PPO and the Genesis physics simulator, running entirely on the Jetson Orin Nano.

item.title: PyTorch Setup on Jetson Orin Nano
item.description:
PyTorch Setup on Jetson Orin Nano

The Jetson Orin Nano uses an ARM64 integrated GPU (nvgpu) with a custom L4T CUDA stack, so standard PyPI torch wheels won't work. This post walks through getting PyTorch running with proper GPU support.

item.title: Enable PREEMPT_RT
item.description:
Enable PREEMPT_RT

This guide covers how to enable PREEMPT_RT on the Jetson Orin Nano, transforming it into a real-time operating system (RTOS) ready for low-latency robotics applications.

item.title: Get Ready!
item.description:
Get Ready!

To start the Jetson Orin Nano journey, the first step is to figure out where to grab the source code and which build tools are needed.

item.title: BitBake recipe pipeline: RunQueue and the Tegra flash artifact
item.description:
BitBake recipe pipeline: RunQueue and the Tegra flash artifact

The previous post, BitBake recipe pipeline: parsing demo-image-weston, ended with the image recipe metadata fully assembled. This post follows the last step. RunQueue expands demo-image-weston:do_build into ordered task execution, then the generated Tegra image task creates the flashable .tegraflash-tar.zst file.

item.title: BitBake recipe pipeline: parsing demo-image-weston
item.description:
BitBake recipe pipeline: parsing demo-image-weston

The previous post, BitBake recipe pipeline: cache build and target resolution, traced how demo-image-weston.bb becomes a provider for the target string demo-image-weston. This post zooms into that one recipe parse. It follows the require and inherit chain that turns a small image recipe into a full task graph with rootfs, image, and Tegra flash packaging tasks.

item.title: BitBake recipe pipeline: cache build and target resolution
item.description:
BitBake recipe pipeline: cache build and target resolution

The previous post, BitBake config pipeline: the bitbake.conf include chain and the complete namespace, ended with a complete global datastore. This post traces the first half of recipe processing. BitBake discovers every recipe file, parses them into a cache, and only then resolves the command line target demo-image-weston.

item.title: BitBake config pipeline: the bitbake.conf include chain and the complete namespace
item.description:
BitBake config pipeline: the bitbake.conf include chain and the complete namespace

The previous post, BitBake config pipeline: the variable store and the layer map, ended with bitbake.conf located via BBPATH and about to be parsed. This post traces what happens inside that parse: how bitbake.conf triggers a chain of four more config files, why local.conf must come first in that chain, how the machine conf sets IMAGE_FSTYPES and KERNEL_DEVICETREE through four nested require statements, how the distro conf sets DISTRO_FEATURES and INIT_MANAGER, what the deferred :append operator means for parse order, and what the namespace contains when parseConfigurationFiles() finally returns.

item.title: BitBake config pipeline: the variable store and the layer map
item.description:
BitBake config pipeline: the variable store and the layer map

The previous post, BitBake startup: the UI handshake and config trigger, traced how the first getVariable command from the UI triggered init_configdata(), which called initConfigurationData(), which called parseBaseConfiguration(), which finally called parseConfigurationFiles(). This post picks up at the first line of that function. It traces what parseConfigurationFiles() starts with, how reading bblayers.conf gives BitBake its map of the layer topology, how the ten layer.conf files fill in the recipe locations and assemble the BBPATH search path, and how that assembled BBPATH is immediately used to locate bitbake.conf.

item.title: BitBake startup: the UI handshake and config trigger
item.description:
BitBake startup: the UI handshake and config trigger

You type bitbake demo-image-weston and press Enter. By the time the two-process startup completes, the server is blocked in a select loop and the UI client is about to enter knotty.main(). This post traces what happens in that handshake: how "demo-image-weston" got into configParams in the first place, what six commands knotty.main() sends to the server, and how the very first getVariable call silently triggers the entire config-loading pipeline.

If you want the story of how that two-process state was reached, the double-fork and daemonization, see BitBake startup: the two-process model.

item.title: BitBake startup: the two-process model
item.description:
BitBake startup: the two-process model

You type bitbake demo-image-weston and press Enter. Before the first recipe is parsed, before any .conf file is touched, something interesting happens at the OS level: the single process you launched splits in two, the second one daemonizes itself, and the two processes spend the rest of the build communicating over a Unix socket.

This post traces that startup sequence from the shell command down to the server's select loop, with every claim linked to the exact source line.

item.title: What is initrd?
item.description:
What is initrd?

If you have ever peeked at /boot/extlinux/extlinux.conf, you probably noticed a line called INITRD. Have you ever wondered what this thing is, what it does, and why we need it?

item.title: Latest Posts
item.description:
Latest Posts

Welcome to my technical blog. Here I document my journey through Robotics, AI, and Embedded Systems.

item.title: About
item.description:
About

Welcome to my technical blog! I'm Rylan Peng, a developer interested in Robotics, Embedded Systems, and AI.

This blog is a place where I share my d...
